coredata.dc:title: The role of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots synthesized from spent lithium-ion batteries for Highly efficient supercapacitors

coredata.dc:description: GO and GQDs synthesized from commercial graphite were named GOcom and GQDscom, respectively. Furthermore, the GO and GQDs synthesized from recycled graphite (extracted from Lithium-ion-batteries) were named GOLIB and GQDsLIB, respectively. Electrochemical tests indicated that graphene supercapacitors (SCs) containing in their electrodes GOcom and GOLIB produced maximum capacitances of 1131.4 F/g and 922.7 F/g, respectively. Those devices also generated energy densities of 33–35 Wh/kg. Later, SCs were made with electrodes containing GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB, consequently, the capacitance was enhanced up to outstanding values of 2130 F/g and 2519.1 F/g, respectively. This means an increase of capacitance by 122 %-131 % after adding the GQDs to the SCs (compared to the devices made with only GO). Surprisingly, energy densities also increased significantly by 263 %-275 % after adding the GQDs to the SCs. Furthermore, these devices exhibit remarkable electrochemical stability, since flexible supercapacitors using GOLIB and GQDsLIB retained 88 % of capacitance after 1000 charge/discharge cycles and over 80 % of capacitance after 500 bending cycles. XPS, Raman and FTIR analyses demonstrated the increment of oxygen vacancies defects and chemical species (peroxides, carboxyl groups, esters, carbonyl and alkoxy) after the introduction of GO and GQDs on the SC electrodes. All those species/defects contributed to the storage of charge by enhancing the conductivity and redox centers on the SC electrodes. Overall, this work demonstrates the promising potential of reusing graphite from spent lithium-ion batteries as a sustainable and effective material for next-generation flexible, high-performance SCs. At the same time, the battery waste is converted into value added products.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: GO and GQDs synthesized from commercial graphite were named GOcom and GQDscom, respectively. Furthermore, the GO and GQDs synthesized from recycled graphite (extracted from Lithium-ion-batteries) were named GOLIB and GQDsLIB, respectively. Electrochemical tests indicated that graphene supercapacitors (SCs) containing in their electrodes GOcom and GOLIB produced maximum capacitances of 1131.4 F/g and 922.7 F/g, respectively. Those devices also generated energy densities of 33–35 Wh/kg. Later, SCs were made with electrodes containing GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB, consequently, the capacitance was enhanced up to outstanding values of 2130 F/g and 2519.1 F/g, respectively. This means an increase of capacitance by 122 %-131 % after adding the GQDs to the SCs (compared to the devices made with only GO). Surprisingly, energy densities also increased significantly by 263 %-275 % after adding the GQDs to the SCs. Furthermore, these devices exhibit remarkable electrochemical stability, since flexible supercapacitors using GOLIB and GQDsLIB retained 88 % of capacitance after 1000 charge/discharge cycles and over 80 % of capacitance after 500 bending cycles. XPS, Raman and FTIR analyses demonstrated the increment of oxygen vacancies defects and chemical species (peroxides, carboxyl groups, esters, carbonyl and alkoxy) after the introduction of GO and GQDs on the SC electrodes. All those species/defects contributed to the storage of charge by enhancing the conductivity and redox centers on the SC electrodes. Overall, this work demonstrates the promising potential of reusing graphite from spent lithium-ion batteries as a sustainable and effective material for next-generation flexible, high-performance SCs. At the same time, the battery waste is converted into value added products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are fundamental in electronic technology, their use extends to numerous portable devices such as mobile phones, tablets, laptops, smart watches and others. Their widespread use lies in several key advantages: high energy density, energy efficiency, long cycle life, stable voltage output and a low self-discharge rate. In recent years, research on LIBs has grown exponentially towards the field of electromobility, especially in the development of batteries for electric vehicles (EVs). The International Energy Agency (https://www.iea.org/) estimated that in 2019, EVs generated approximately 500,000 tons of LIB waste. In fact, it is expected to exceed 8 million tons by 2040. Furthermore, in 2017 global EV sales reached 3 million units, which increased to 26 million by 2024. According to future projections, this number could reach 125 million by 2030 and 530 million by 2040 [1]. Then, the accelerated growth in the use of LIBs implicates significant environmental challenges, above all, the extraction of raw materials and the environmental management of batteries at the end of their lifetime. It must be taken into consideration that the battery modules in EVs have an average useful life of 8 years. In addition, the weight that the batteries have in an EV is very significant, for example, the Tesla Model 3 Long Range contains 4416 cells that form the battery module, and it weighs a total of 480 kg [2]. The European Union (EU) has implemented initiative Nr.2019/1020, which establishes that, by January 1st 2030, 95 % of cobalt, copper and nickel, as well as 70 % of lithium from new batteries, must come from recycling LIBs materials [3]. This regulation promotes a circular economy in the battery industry and mitigates the environmental impact associated with its production and disposal. Currently, there are established processes for recycling batteries and recovering their metals, the most used are pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical processes, or a combination of both. These processes have a high recovery rate (<90 %) of metals as Co, Ni and Cu. However, they have several environmental disadvantages. In the case of the pyrometallurgical process, there is an emission of gases because the battery waste is directly exposed to high temperatures (500 – 2000 °C) to incinerate the organic matter (plastics and binder) and recover the metals in the form of alloys. On the other hand, the hydrometallurgical process involves the use of acids, bases, or chemical solvents, which creates an environmental problem due to the secondary waste generated. Another disadvantage of these processes is the number of stages required before obtaining the recovered metals [4]. The cathode and anode of LIBs account for approximately 50 % of their total weight, while the electrolyte constitutes 10 %, copper and aluminum current collectors add another 10 %, and the remainder consists of binders, additives, separators, and cell casing. In the anode, the primary active materials are graphite and hard carbon, whose recovery has gained significant interest in the industry due to their potential for advanced applications [5].

Until now, just a few works have focused on the use of hazardous waste as a raw material for the synthesis of advanced materials with applications in energy storage. One promising strategy involves transforming these hazardous residues into carbon-based materials with porous structures and heteroatom doping. Those last characteristics are useful to increase the electrochemical performance of energy storage devices. Some scarce examples about the use of carbon waste for the fabrication of supercapacitors include: Yang et al. [6] synthesized sulfur-doped mesoporous carbon fibers from coal pitch (a byproduct of coal processing [6]), this carbon material exhibited a capacitance and energy density of 233 F/g and 27.92 Wh/kg, respectively. Also, it showed a high cycling stability of 92 % after 3000 cycles of charge-discharge. Another material is the porous carbon nanorods derived from the pyrolysis oil of discarded tires [7], this hierarchical porous structure has a surface area of 1448 m2/g and produces a capacitance and energy density of 185 F/g and 36.8 Wh/kg respectively. Furthermore, it has been reported nitrogen-doped porous carbon synthesized from pitch industry waste for energy storage application [8]. This porous carbon structure exhibited a high surface area (2020 m2/g) and produced a high capacitance of 220 F/g as well as energy density of 27.92 Wh/kg. Thus, the works mentioned above not only contribute to mitigating the environmental impact of hazardous waste but also promote the circular economy by integrating them into the manufacture of energy storage devices such as supercapacitors. Then, the processing of carbon-based waste to make energy storage devices is promising. Multiple approaches to recover graphite from spent batteries and to transform it into carbon nanostructures with enhanced properties have been reported [9]. For instance, Yu et al. [10] synthesized graphene oxide (GO) from graphite recovered from LIBs using the modified Hummer´s method. This oxidative process effectively removes organic and inorganic impurities from graphite layers, producing a nanomaterial free of surface contaminants. Similarly, Zhang et al. [11] used graphite recovered from LIBs and applied Hummer´s method to synthesize GO, focusing on the analysis of oxygen functional groups and structural defects formed during synthesis. These characteristics are critical for energy storage applications, as they facilitate electrolyte interaction with the material's surface, thereby enhancing their performance. A significant portion of research on recovered graphite transformed into GO has been directed toward water remediation applications, exploding the large surface area and surface modifications of the nanomaterial achieved during Hummer´s method. These studies have demonstrated GO's effectiveness in removing contaminants such as heavy metals (As [12] and Pb [13]) and organic compounds like the antibiotic tetracycline from wastewater [14].

On the other hand, energy storage applications for GO or reduced GO (rGO) synthesized from graphite recovered from used batteries remain limited. Roy et al. [15] for example, reported only the capacitance properties of GO and rGO, obtaining low capacitances of 1.8 µF/g and 48 µF/g (at 1 mA/g), respectively. This study highlights the synthesis of graphene from wasted zinc–carbon batteries, where graphite powder is recovered via acid treatment and used to produce GO using Hummers' method. Similarly, Perumal et al. [16] found that rGO and thermally treated rGO (T-rGO) derived from recovered graphite produced low capacitances of 136 F/g and 84 F/g, respectively. Also, energy densities at same current were reported as 12.1Wh/kg and 7.5 Wh/kg using a 1 M Na₂SO₄ solution at 1 A/g. In contrast, Adaikalam et al. [17] investigated the use of unmodified recovered graphite, subjected only to washing and milling for direct application in supercapacitors. Additionally, CaO particles were incorporated to enhance capacitance, achieving values of 13.8 F/g for the graphite/CaO composite and 12.9 F/g for pure CaO. However, specific analyses revealed that graphite’s contribution to energy storage was minimal (0.9 F/g). These findings highlight the importance of converting recovered graphite into nanomaterials such as GO or rGO to significantly improve the capacitive performance and electrochemical properties of carbon materials, this in turn, promotes the applications of these materials in energy storage devices.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a zero-dimensional carbon allotrope characterized by lateral dimensions of less than 100 nm and a thickness of fewer than 10 layers. They retain the laminar structure and hexagonal lattice parameters of graphene, which distinguishes them from carbon dots (CDs) that may exhibit similar sizes but possess an amorphous structure [18]. Due to their nanoscale size, GQDs exhibit unique physical and chemical properties, making them ideal for a wide range of applications, including biomedicine, biosensing, photonics, photovoltaics, and optoelectronics [19,20]. Despite these advantages, the low yield of GQD synthesis has limited their exploration in energy storage applications, nevertheless, recent studies have demonstrated the exceptional electrochemical performance of GQDs [21]. To address the challenges associated with their integration into energy storage devices such as supercapacitors, a promising strategy involves the development of nanocomposites by combining GQDs with other materials such as GO, rGO, CNTs and metallic nanoparticles [22]. For example, Zhang et al. [23] synthesized GQDs with uniform sizes for application in energy storage. The GQD based electrode stored charge by electric double-layer capacitance (EDLC) and had a capacitance of 296.7 F/g, energy density (Ed) of 41.2 Wh/kg and power density (Pd) of 500 W/kg at 1A/g in 2 M KOH. In contrast, rGO exhibits a lower electrochemical performance of 135.4 F/g, 18.8 Wh/kg and 400 W/kg, respectively. Additionally, the electrode showed a capacitance retention above 90 % after 5000 cycles. Moreover, Zhang et al. [24] made a composite using hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets (HPCNs) and GQDs. The sample named HPCN-1:3 exhibited the highest capacitance of 200 F/g (at 1A/g) in 6 M KOH. Also, it illustrated capacitance retention above 90 % after 10 000 cycles of charge-discharge. Those authors didn´t report the values of energy and power density. Tan et al. [25] prepared conductive porous carbon decorated with GQDs and evaluated its electrochemical performance for energy storage application, the nanocomposite showed high capacitances of 315 F/g, notable energy density of 9.21 Wh/kg and power density of 247.75 W/kg (at 1 A/g) in 6 M KOH. Furthermore, the electrode material shows excellent cycle stability with no decrease of capacitance after 10,000 cycles of charge-discharge (at 10 A/g). Another work by Chen et al. [26] deposited GQDs onto the three-dimensional graphene (3DG), the rigid supercapacitor fabricated with GQD–3DG exhibited a high capacitance of 268 F/g, representing more than 90 % improvement over that SC made from pure 3DG electrodes (136 F/g), for energy density the values reported were 23.8 Wh/kg and 12.1 Wh/kg, the measurements were done at 1.25 A/g in 1 M H2SO4. Also, the device retained 90 % of its initial capacitance after 5000 charging–discharging cycles. Finally, Zhao et al. [27] reported a simple strategy to integrate GQDs into carbon nanofibers (CNFs). The nanocomposite named AGRCNF-3 shows a high capacitance of 335 F/g (at 1 A/g) in 6 M KOH, also the flexible supercapacitor fabricated exhibits an energy density of 3.73 Wh/kg and a power density of 34.02 kW/kg. Furthermore, the device maintains its capacitance retention above 93 % after 10,000 cycles of charge-discharge (at 50 A/g).

Due to the need of GQDs based SC with higher capacitances/energy-densities at low cost, in this study, we present an innovative process for obtaining carbon nanomaterials, specifically, graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from wasted graphite, which was recovered from spent lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Our method is distinguished by its simplicity, effectively minimizing the number of processing steps compared to the multi-step approaches, commonly reported in the literature. Additionally, this is the first work to simultaneously produce GO and GQDs from recycled graphite (taken from LIBs), incorporated into flexible supercapacitors. Also, a systematic comparison was made between nanomaterials synthesized from commercial graphite and those derived from lithium-ion battery (LIB) residues. The electrochemical performance of the devices fabricated with GO or GQDs synthesized from graphite recovered from LIBs (GOLIB-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC) was compared with devices made with GO or GQDs derived from commercial graphite (referred to as GOcom-SC and GOcom+GQDscom-SC). The results demonstrated that the flexible devices fabricated using commercial graphite, GOcom-SC and GOcom+GQDscom-SC, achieved specific capacitance values of 1131.4 F/g and 2519.1 F/g, respectively. Then, the incorporation of GQDs increased energy storage capacity by 123 %. A similar trend was observed in devices based on graphite from LIBs (GOLIB-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC), which exhibited capacitance values of 922.7 F/g and 2130.2 F/g, respectively, representing a 131 % increase with the addition of GQDs to the GOLIB. Comparing GOcom-SC and GOLIB-SC devices, where the only difference is the type of graphite, a 19.5 % reduction in electrochemical performance was observed, however, the GOLIB-SC device still demonstrates significant potential for energy storage applications, showcasing its viability as a sustainable alternative. In supercapacitors made with GQDs (GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC), this decrease was limited to 15.4 %. These results suggest that recycled graphite from LIBs is a viable and sustainable alternative for the fabrication of supercapacitors, maintaining competitive electrochemical properties. Finally, for the devices incorporating GQDs (GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC), which exhibited the best performance, their chemical stability properties were studied by subjecting the devices to 1000 charge/discharge cycle and the results indicated that both devices maintained high capacitance retention, with values of 91.0 % and 88.5 % for GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC, respectively. Those devices were also subjected to 500 bending cycles and displayed capacitance retention of 84.1 % and 79.3 % for GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC devices, respectively.

This work presents the following novelties: 1) the first report on the synthesis of both carbon nanostructures (GO and GQDs) from graphite recycled from spent lithium batteries, 2) a detailed analysis of the relevance of GQDs in energy storage, 3) development of the first flexible and high-performance devices fabricated with GO and GQDs derived from graphite recycled from spent lithium batteries, 4) we reported the highest capacitance (2519.1 F/g) and energy density (126 Wh/kg) for a flexible device made from a hazardous waste and 5) we report a viable alternative to reduce the waste generated by lithium-ion batteries by transforming them into materials for energy storage devices, which has been scarcely reported in the literature. Therefore, this research demonstrates that it is possible to fabricate flexible SC devices incorporating carbon materials synthesized from LIB waste. This strategy provides a viable option to reduce the annual waste generated by electronic devices. The methods for recycling graphite proposed here could be applied to synthesize carbon compounds from spent batteries already used in electric vehicles. This is important because recycling compounds from batteries employed in electric cars will be mandatory a need in the near future (provoked by the scarcity).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide from commercial graphite (GOcom) and graphite recycled from spent lithium batteries (GOLIB)
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized using the Hummers method [28]. The procedure for the recovery of graphite from LIBs was explained in section S1 of supporting information. The procedure started by dispersing 2 g of commercial graphite in 46 mL of H₂SO₄ in ice bath under constant stirring. Subsequently, 6 g of KMnO₄ was slowly added to the mixture solution, ensuring that the temperature did not exceed 20 °C. After completing the addition and homogenizing the mixture, the ice bath was removed, and the solution was heated to 35 °C to initiate the reaction for 2 h. After this time, 92 mL of distilled water was added slowly. The mixture was transferred to a beaker, where a solution of distilled water and H₂O₂ (27:1) was added. The GO was then washed with a 2.5 % v/v HCl solution and centrifuged to remove dissolved metal ions. This washing process was repeated until a neutral pH was achieved. The final mixture was diluted with distilled water to adjust the GO concentration to 3 mg/mL. Finally, the solution was exfoliated for 3 h in an ultrasonic bath, ensuring the temperature did not exceed 50 °C to prevent chemical changes in the carbon material. This procedure was carried out using commercial graphite, and the graphene oxide obtained was named GOcom. Additionally, the same Hummers method was utilized but graphite extracted from used lithium batteries was employed, and the graphene oxide synthesized in this case was labeled as GOLIB. The experimental details in supplementary information (section S1) describe the procedure to obtain graphite from lithium batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dos (GQDs) from commercial graphite and graphite recycled from lithium batteries
ce:section.ce:para: First, 100 mL of GO solution was sonicated for 3 h, then 170 mL of distilled water and 30 mL of NH4OH were added to the mixture and this one was sonicated for 3 more hours. After this, the dispersion was centrifuged for 15 min at 3000 G. In order to separate the GQDs for the dispersion, 10 mL of the stable dispersion were placed in 15 ml falcon tubes with 0.1 M of ascorbic acid and shaken manually for a few seconds. Then, samples were centrifuged for 20 min at 2900 G, and the GQDs remained stable in the upper phase separated from the precipitated material. The synthesis and production of GQDs were carried out using the previously mentioned graphene oxide samples. The GQDs derived from GOcom were named GQDscom, while those obtained from GOLIB were labeled as GQDsLIB. Figure S1 shows the general process for the recovery of graphite from LIBs and its use for the synthesis of carbon nanostructures (GO and QGDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of supercapacitors (SCs) made with graphene oxide and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: This work studied the effect of incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into graphene oxide sheets to form nanocomposites using two different graphite sources: commercial graphite and graphite recovered from lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). The article discusses first the electrochemical performance of the electrodes GOcom electrode, GOLIB electrode, GOcom+GQDscom electrode and GOLIB+GQDsLIB electrode. Later, it was discussed the performance of the GOcom-SC, GOcom+GQDscom-SC, GOLIB-SC, and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC devices. Table S1 in supporting information describes the characteristics of both the electrodes and the devices. The SCs were fabricated using a scalable and fast methodology. First, polymeric substrates (recycled polyethylene (PE) plastic with dimensions of 2.5 cm x 2.5 cm) were cleaned with soap and water, then rinsed with distilled water, ethyl alcohol, and isopropyl alcohol via ultrasonication for 10 min. Subsequently, the cleaned substrates dried at room temperature. For GOcom-SC and GOLIB-SC devices, the electrodes were fabricated using a drop-coating technique. First, the polymeric substrates were covered with a thin film of conductive graphite, then, 1 mL of graphene oxide was deposited onto the conductive substrate and heated at 60 °C for 10 min to evaporate the solvent and form a uniform film on the electrode. A semi-permeable, absorbent, and biodegradable cellulose membrane (separator) was moistened with an H₃PO₄/PVA/H₂O (2/0.1/1) electrolyte. The separator was placed between two SC electrodes, forming a sandwich structure. A Cu current collector was then attached to the SC electrodes using an Ag ink, and the edges were sealed using silicone. The same procedure was used to fabricate GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC devices. However, in these cases, the electrode was prepared by mixing 1 mL of GO with 1 mL of GQDs before deposition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of electrodes made with graphene oxide (GO) and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrodes fabricated with carbon nanomaterials were characterized using various techniques. The morphology of the electrodes was analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, model Quanta 250), operated at 1.0 kV to observe the structure of graphene oxide mixed with GQDs on the SC electrodes. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, model Thermo Scientific) was employed to identify the chemical elements present on the electrodes. The structural properties of the materials were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8) in a 2θ range of 10–80°, with Cu-kα radiation (λ=1.5418 Å) at a scanning speed of 0.1 °/s. UV–vis measurements were carried out using a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 900 spectrophotometer in the range of 250–800 nm. FTIR spectra were recorded in the range of 450–4000 cm⁻¹ with an ATR accessory using a Perkin-Elmer spectrometer. For X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), the samples were analyzed using a Thermo Scientific K-alpha system with an AlKα (1486.68 eV) X-ray source. Raman spectroscopy measurements were performed using a Renishaw micro-Raman system with a 532 nm excitation laser under ambient conditions. In this study, the spectra were obtained from 10 representative measurements recorded at different points on the samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of supercapacitors made with graphene oxide and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical measurements, cyclic-voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic-charging/discharging (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed on electrochemical equipment (Wavenow potentiostat/galvanostat). CV measurements were recorded in scan rates of 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 mV/s in the working voltage window of 0.0 – 0.8 V with 2-electrodes configuration in H3PO4/H2O electrolyte. GCD curves were measured at three different current densities 1, 5 and 10 A/g at 0.0 – 0.8 V voltage window for GOcom-SC and GOLIB-SC devices and 0.0 - 1.2 V for GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC. Finally, EIS tests were performed by utilizing an AC voltage with 0.03 mV amplitude in the frequency range of 10 Hz-100 kHz and under open circuit-potential condition. The capacitance and energy density were calculated for the SCs utilizing the procedure explained in section S2, see supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural properties of electrodes made with graphene oxide and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a-1b present SEM images of the surface morphology of the GOcom and GOLIB electrodes. Both samples exhibit a continuous graphene oxide (GO) film uniformly coating the conductive graphite ink substrate. Higher-magnification images, shown in Figures S2a and S2b, reveal the characteristic wrinkled morphology of the graphene oxide sheets [29]. This wrinkling is attributed to hydrogen interactions with the abundant carboxylic groups formed on the surface during the modified Hummers’ method. Additionally, compressive stress generated during water evaporation in the GO film deposition process further accentuates this wrinkling [30]. In energy storage applications, this wrinkled morphology is advantageous as it increases the available surface area and facilitates electrolyte penetration through the "microchannels" formed by the folds. These structural features contribute to improved electrochemical performance, enhancing the accessibility of active sites and ion transport within the electrode material.

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) synthesized in this study are derived from graphene oxide. Their optical property as photoluminescence arises from the quantum confinement effect inherent to the nanostructure, distinguishing them from conventional graphene, where that phenomena do not appear due to its larger size [31]. The reduction in size, edge defects in the nanostructure, doping with heteroatoms, and the presence of functional groups on the surface of the GQDs induce changes in the electronic band energy of the material [32]. These modifications enable photoluminescence upon energy irradiation.

Figures S3a-S3b in supporting information show the photoluminescence spectra of GQDs synthesized from commercial graphite (Figure S3a) and graphite recovered from lithium-ion batteries, see Figure S3b. Both spectra in Figures S3a and S3b exhibit similar photoluminescence, with an emission range covering from 350 to 700 nm. The maximum emission for GQDs derived from commercial graphite (GQDscom) is observed at 465 nm with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, whereas for GQDs obtained from LIB graphite (GQDsLIB), the maximum emission occurs at 420 nm with an excitation wavelength of 515 nm. This shift in the emission region of GQDs is associated with variations in the size of the nanomaterials. Previous studies have reported a strong correlation between the size of GQDs and their luminescent emission [33]. Reports indicate that for GQDs with sizes ranging from 10–15 nm, the photoluminescence maximum occurs at 465 nm [34]. Additionally, an increase in size to a range of 20–30 nm causes a shift of the main emission band to 520 nm [35]. Therefore, in our study, based on the obtained emission wavelength, the GQDscom and GQDsLIB samples have different sizes. It is important to note that not only size affects the emission wavelength of GQDs, but they are also strongly influenced by surface defects, such as oxygen vacancies or the incorporation of atoms into the nanostructure [36]. To confirm the relation size vs emission wavelength, dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were conducted. The size distribution histograms are presented in Figures S3c-S3d. GQDscom have an average size of 27.8 nm with a narrow distribution ranging from 20 to 35 nm. In contrast, GQDsLIB have an average size of 68.7 nm, with a broader distribution from 50 to 110 nm. These size differences account for the observed shifts in the emission properties of the samples. Finally, insets in Figures S3c-S3d display digital photographs of the GQDs samples under excitation with an external laser source at 358 nm. The observed luminescence in both materials confirms the presence of GQDs and their optical properties, providing visual evidence of light emissions.

Fig. 1c-1d display SEM images of the surface of the GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB electrodes. In both images, flat sheets fully covering the electrode surface can be observed, corresponding to graphene oxide (GO). Additionally, the surface of the sheets is covered with fragments identified as GQDs adhered to the GO sheets. The inset SEM images in the main figures provide a closer view of the surface, where the GQDs can be clearly distinguished. As previously mentioned, GQDs synthesized from commercial graphite and battery residues exhibit differences in size, which are clearly visible in the images. Using the ImageJ software and the SEM images, the GQDs com were measured to have an average size of 47.5 nm, whereas the GQDs LIB showed a larger average size of 67.6 nm. These results will later be compared with those obtained by DLS, which indicated larger sizes for GQDs LIB. Furthermore, the inset of Fig. 1d reveals larger particles, approximately 100 nm in size. Moreover, Fig. 1e-1f present the EDS spectra of the GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB electrodes. The EDS spectra provide information about the chemical composition of the electrodes. Table S2 in supporting information presents the quantitative chemical compositions for the GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB electrodes. A comparison of the EDS spectra for both electrodes reveals a similar content of carbon (64–65 %), suggesting that a similar content of carbon is obtained for the SC electrodes despite the source of graphite (commercial or recycled) utilized for the synthesis of the GQDS. The content (wt %) of N and S was also similar for both electrodes, see table S2 in the supporting information. These findings highlight two critical points: Firstly, one of the advantages of the Hummers method is the fact that it allows the production of carbon nanomaterials with high purity despite the use of recycled graphite (from spent LIBs) as raw material. Thus, both commercial and recycled graphite are useful for the synthesis of GO sheets. Secondly, the material derived from LIB residues does not contain elements such as Mn, Co, Ni, or Li, typically found in lithium batteries. Thus, the electrochemical performance of the electrodes will not be influenced by additional redox-active species.

Fig. 2 shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the electrodes used for the fabrication of the supercapacitors (SCs). Fig. 2a and b display the diffraction patterns of electrodes coated exclusively with GO derived from commercial graphite and lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), respectively. Fig. 2d and e correspond to the diffraction patterns of electrodes made with GO and GQDs, also derived from commercial graphite and LIBs. As observed all those patterns show the typical diffraction peaks associated to graphene oxide. Lastly, Fig. 2c and f present the diffraction patterns of the flexible substrate used, which consists of high-density polyethylene (HDPE) [37]. In the diffraction patterns of samples with GO and GO + GQDs, the predominant peaks are attributed to the flexible substrate. However, in the 13–15° region, an additional peak corresponding to GO is observed, marked with a diamond symbol. Fig. 3 focuses on this specific region (13–15°), highlighting the diffraction patterns of electrodes with GO (Fig. 3a and b) and GO + GQDs (Fig. 3c and d). In all cases, a broad peak is observed, with the maximum centered between 13.92 and 14.04° Using Bragg’s Law, the interplanar distance of the materials in the electrodes was calculated, yielding values between 0.630 nm and 0.636 nm. These minor variations indicate that the deposition of carbon nanomaterials on the flexible substrate did not cause significant re-stacking of the carbon layers. It is noteworthy that the interplanar distance in graphite structure is 0.336 nm [38]. Thus, these results suggest that the carbon layers in the electrodes remain separated, which it means an increasing of 88 – 90 % in the interplanar distance over graphite, this will facilitate the penetration of the electrolyte between the layers when used in SC devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of graphene oxide and GQDs based supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 displays the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of electrodes based on GO and GO+GQDs obtained from commercial graphite and lithium-ion battery (LIB) residues. These measurements were conducted in a two-electrode electrochemical cell configuration, using a gel of H₃PO₄/H₂O/PVA (3:1:0.1) as electrolyte. The scan rates were 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 mV/s, covering a potential window of 0–800 mV. Fig. 4a and b, correspond to electrodes coated with GO alone (GOcom and GOLIB), exhibit similar behavior characterized by a rectangular shape, indicative of an electric double-layer capacitance (EDLC) storage energy mechanism. This mechanism involves ion adsorption on the electrode surface due to polarization, without electrochemical reactions and characteristic of carbon materials [39]. In contrast, Fig. 4 and d show the CV curves of GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB electrodes, where shoulders are observed, indicating redox reactions. This behavior is attributed to surface defects generated during GQD synthesis. The redox sites significantly enhance the energy storage, with maximum currents increasing from 50 mA/g (GOcom) and 30 mA/g (GOLIB) to 250 mA/g and 200 mA/g, for GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB electrodes respectively, representing increases of 400 % and 560 %. Fig. 5 presents galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves for the same electrodes. Fig. 5 and b, corresponding to GOcom and GOLIB, exhibit rapid exponential energy release characteristic of the EDLC mechanism, at a current of 1A/g the operating voltage is 0 V after 4 h. In contrast, Fig. 5c and d, incorporating GQDs, demonstrate gradual energy release with maximum discharge times of 8–9 h at 10 A/g, compared to 4 h for electrodes without GQDs.

Subsequently, flexible supercapacitors were fabricated based on the analysis of the materials in an electrochemical cell, GOcom-SC and GOLIB-SC (GO only), as well as GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC (with GQDs) were measured utilizing the previously mentioned H3PO4 electrolyte. The CV curves in Fig. 6 reveal EDLC behavior in solid state devices without GQDs (Fig. 6a and b). These curves show no evidence of redox reactions, consistent with measurements in the electrochemical cell, confirming the energy storage mechanism as EDLC. Contrasted, devices incorporating GQDs (Fig. 6c and d) exhibit banana-shaped curves. This shape change, without redox shoulders, is attributed to the "sandwich" configuration of the device, where all components (electrolyte, separator, current collector, binder, and material thickness) generate a resistance that influences the electrochemical performance [40]. The measurements conducted in an electrochemical cell predominantly focus on the electrode surface and its associated redox reactions, as this configuration cell exhibits heightened sensitivity to current flow and electrolyte exposure [41]. In contrast, the present study evaluates the complete device, effectively simulating real-world operating conditions. Despite that, devices with GQDs show a 300 % increase in maximum current density. Finally, Fig. 7 presents GCD curves for flexible devices. GOcom-SC and GOLIB-SC devices (Fig. 7a and b) exhibit operating voltages of 0.33 V and 0.26 V after 10 h, respectively. Compared to the devices with GQDs, GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC (Fig. 7c and d), achieve voltages of 1.40 V and 1.22 V, corresponding to increases of 325 % and 370 % in final operating voltage over the same period (10 h). Using GCD curves from Fig. 7, capacitance and energy density values of the devices were calculated using formulas 1 and 2 of the supplementary information S2 and the results are shown in Table 1 .

The devices without GQDs (GOcom-SC and GOLIB-SC) exhibit moderate capacitance values, characteristic of an energy storage mechanism based on electrical double-layer capacitance (EDLC). The capacitance difference between GOcom-SC and GOLIB-SC is 18.5 %, while the difference in energy density is only 4 %. These results suggest that recycled graphite is a viable raw material for producing graphene oxide (GO) for use in supercapacitors (SCs). On the other hand, the highest capacitance and energy density values were observed in devices made with GQDs (GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC). The results demonstrate a significant increase in capacitance: 122 % for GOcom-SC/GOcom+GQDscom-SC and 131 % for GOLIB-SC/GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC. This improvement can be attributed to the surface defects introduced during GQD synthesis, which facilitate redox reactions, enhancing the energy storage capacity. It is worth noting that while both materials (GOcom and GOLIB) are viable for SC applications, the combination of GO from commercial graphite with GQDs (GOcom+GQDscom-SC) slightly outperforms the GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC device, with 14 % higher capacitance and 1 % higher energy density. Nevertheless, recycled graphite from LIBs remains a sustainable and efficient option for future SC fabrication.

The efficiency of supercapacitor devices is fundamentally influenced by the resistance at the interfaces between the electrolyte, electrode, and current collector. Lower resistance enhances energy storage capacitance and facilitates efficient electron and ion transport within the system, thereby improving overall device performance. In the context of supercapacitors, two critical resistance parameters, series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct), play pivotal roles in determining electrochemical performance. Rs involves the combined resistances of the electrolyte, current collectors, and the contact interface between the active material and the electrode. A low Rs is desirable as it minimizes energy losses during charge and discharge cycles, thereby improving the coulombic efficiency of the device. On the other hand, high Rs can lead to significant voltage drops, increased heat generation, and reduced device longevity. Optimizing the conductivity of the electrolyte and ensuring good contact between the active material and electrode are essential for minimizing Rs. Rct represents the resistance to electron transfer occurring at the interface between the electrode material and the electrolyte. This parameter is particularly critical in supercapacitors utilizing faradaic processes, where redox reactions are key to energy storage. High Rct can slow down these reactions, thereby limiting the charge/discharge rates and reducing the energy density of the device. Strategies to lower Rct include using highly conductive electrode materials, optimizing pore structures for ion accessibility, and employing surface modifications to enhance electrochemical activity [42].

After a comparison of our results of capacitance with these in Table 2, we demonstrated that the GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC devices exhibit superior supercapacitive performance compared to previously reported studies in the literature, particularly those synthesizing GQDs from waste-derived carbon sources. For instance, when compared to the study by Gupta et al. [43], the GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC devices show capacitance improvements of approximately 11.5-fold and 9.5-fold, respectively. It is important to note that the capacitance reported by Gupta corresponds solely to GQDs. In our study, the incorporation of GO into the system plays a pivotal role by providing structural support for the GQDs, creating a synergistic interaction between the materials that significantly enhances performance. The electrochemical performance of the flexible device GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC with a capacitance of 2130 F/g, surpasses comparable studies by factors of 6.8 [48], 20 [49], and 46 [50] times, which incorporate exfoliated graphite or carbon nanostructures as rGO derived from LIB waste. Furthermore, unlike most previous reports, which focus solely on electrochemical properties in a three-electrode configuration, our study successfully demonstrates the fabrication and performance evaluation of a flexible device. Also, it is important to highlight that this study represents the first report of GO and GQDs synthesis from recycled graphite obtained from used lithium batteries. Furthermore, it is the first publication of a flexible device fabricated using both carbon nanostructures derived from hazardous waste, as opposed to numerous reports that source carbon nanostructures from biomass residues. Additionally, the proposed graphite recycling process does not require specialized reagents or involve energy-intensive carbonization, which translates into a significant cost reduction. These features make the method proposed here highly appealing for industrial scaling.

The GO and GQDs (synthesized from graphite recovered from spent lithium-ion batteries) could be fabricated in mass production. The Hummers method is considered a low-cost process and can be adapted to industrial-scale reactors [51]. However, this is not the only viable route for the mass production of materials with application in energy storage devices. Ma et al. [52] reported a large-scale method for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene (N-graphene) by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) in a fluidized-bed reactor, using ethylene (C₂H₄) as the carbon source and ammonia (NH₃) as the doping agent. This procedure not only allowed the continuous and homogeneous production of N-doped graphene, but also it had a high electrochemical performance, since it produced a specific capacitance of 231 F/g and an energy density of 128 Wh/kg. From a preliminary cost-benefit analysis, the most significant economic advantage is the utilization of wasted graphite, specifically, graphite recovered from spent lithium-ion batteries. Using recycled graphite instead of commercial graphite for the synthesis of carbon nanostructures can reduce costs by up to 95 % as reported by Xie et al. [53]. Those authors conducted a comparative cost analysis between wasted graphite (provided by industries) and synthetic graphite purchased from Betray New Energy Materials Co. Although additional purification and post-treatment steps introduce some operational costs, the overall process remains economically favorable for wasted graphite. In fact, such a study reported a net profit of approximately $5134.72 per ton of reused graphite compared to the commercial graphite. Hence, our own findings demonstrate that GOLIB derived from wasted graphited exhibits high electrochemical performance as mentioned above, which makes it competitive for energy storage applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the electrochemical stability of GO and GQDs based supercapacitors

ce:section.ce:para: The GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC devices offers several advantages compared to traditional devices, which often face issues of low electrochemical performance, rigidity, and cycling stability. Our devices effectively address these problems, since the GOcom+GQDs-SC and GOLIB+GQDs-SC devices demonstrates an excellent lifecycle. As shown in Fig. 9 a, after 1000 charge/discharge cycles, the capacitance retention for both devices are 91.0 % and 88.5 %, respectively. Inset in Fig. 9a shows a picture of a typical SC in flat state. It is noteworthy that both devices exhibit remarkable stability throughout their operational lifespan, with the materials demonstrating negligible degradation during charge/discharge processes. The capacitance retention change after 1000 cycles is less than 3 %, indicating that there is no significant difference in performance over extended cycling. These results highlight the reliability and durability of the materials used in these devices.

The GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC devices were specifically engineered for flexible energy storage applications, making mechanical stability a critical attribute. To evaluate their performance under deformation, both devices were subjected to repeated bending cycles at a 45° angle, with electrochemical capacitance determined every 100 cycles (inset in Fig. 9b shows a SC device in bent state). As shown in Fig. 9b, after 500 bending cycles, the GOcom+GQDscom-SC device retained 84.1 % of its initial capacitance, while the GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC device demonstrated a slightly lower retention of 79.3 %. These results confirm that both devices exhibit robust mechanical and electrochemical stability under mechanical stress. Such stability highlights their potential for integration into a wide range of flexible applications, including wearable electronics like smartwatches and flexible smartphones.

To elucidate the synergy between graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and their exceptional performance in energy storage applications, several characterizations of the electrodes were performed before and after their use in supercapacitors. Among these, UV–vis spectroscopy was employed, as shown in Fig. 10 , to analyze defect-related features. It is well established that an increase in absorbance is indicative of a higher density of defects in the material. Measurements were conducted on 1) electrodes coated exclusively with GO (black curve), 2) electrodes incorporating GO and GQDs before use in SCs (red curve) and 3) electrodes incorporating GO and GQDs after use in SCs (blue curve). Furthermore, these measurements were carried out for materials derived from both commercial graphite (Fig. 10a) and graphite from lithium-ion batteries (Fig. 10b). In both cases, the results displayed similar trends. Electrodes prepared solely with GO (GOcom and GOLIB) exhibited the lowest absorbance. In carbon-based materials such as GO, absorbance is directly related to the number of defects generated during synthesis via the Hummers method. The primary defects include the disruption of the sp² carbon network and its transformation into sp³ due to the incorporation of oxygen functional groups [54]. When GQDs were incorporated, a notable increase in absorbance was observed. This enhancement can be attributed to the high density of defects introduced during the synthesis of GQDs. Specifically, the ultrasonic fragmentation of GO sheets creates significant edge defects in the GQDs and induces a degree of functionalization due to interactions with the solvent used during synthesis [55]. Finally, the electrodes analyzed after their use in SCs showed the highest absorbance with a predominant band centered at 245 nm associated with the CC bonds of the carbon nanostructure and the π-π electronic transitions [56]. This increase is associated with the generation of additional surface defects during electrochemical cycling. The flow of current and interaction with the acidic electrolyte contribute to surface modifications, further increasing defect density. These results underscore the critical role of defect engineering in optimizing the performance of GO and GQDs for energy storage applications.

To further investigate lattice defects, a Raman spectroscopic study was conducted on the GO and GQD-coated electrodes (Fig. 11 ). Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool for characterizing graphene-based materials, providing insights into their crystal lattice structure. The Raman spectra of graphene materials typically exhibit two prominent bands: D band (∼1320 cm⁻¹) arises from structural defects and disorder within the carbon lattice, including edges, vacancies, and functional groups and G band (∼1580 cm⁻¹) is associated with the vibrational mode of sp²-hybridized carbon atoms, reflecting the graphitic order of the material [57]. A widely used parameter for assessing the defect density in carbon materials is the ID/IG ratio, where ID and IG represent the intensities of the D and G bands, respectively [58]. An increase in the ID/IG ratio indicates a higher density of defects in the material, while a lower ratio corresponds to a more ordered lattice. Initially, the GOcom and GOLIB electrodes (Fig. 11a and b) exhibited ID/IG ratios of 0.673 and 0.438, respectively, indicating the presence of a moderate number of defects. These defects are primarily attributed to functional groups on the surface of the graphene oxide sheets, introduced during material synthesis. After the incorporation of GQDs (Fig. 11c and d), the ID/IG ratios increased to 0.892 and 0.798 for the GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB electrodes, respectively. This corresponds to increases of 33 % and 82 %, which are attributed to the intrinsic defects of the GQDs, including edge defects and functionalized sites. Also, the electrodes used in supercapacitors (Fig. 11e and f), the ID/IG ratios further increased to 1.315 and 1.179 for the GOcom+GQDscom-SC and GOLIB+GQDsLIB-SC electrodes, reflecting additional increases of 47 % and 48 %, respectively. This increase is due to the generation of new surface defects during electrochemical cycling, driven by current flow and interaction with the acidic electrolyte. These findings strongly suggest that the exceptional electrochemical performance of the devices is directly linked to the density and nature of defects present. The controlled introduction and evolution of defects, as observed in this report, enhances charge storage mechanisms by providing additional active sites and facilitating ion transport.

The chemical analysis of electrode surfaces is critical for understanding the energy storage mechanism. For this, FTIR (see Figure S4 in supporting information) and XPS (Fig. 12 ) measurements were conducted. The FTIR spectra of GOcom and GOLIB electrodes (Figures S4a-S4d) exhibit characteristic features of graphene oxide (GO). A broad and pronounced band in the 3600–3000 cm⁻¹ range corresponds to the vibrations of -OH bonds associated with the oxidized surface of GO. Additionally, weaker bands observed in the range of 1700–1600 cm⁻¹ region are attributed to the vibrations of C=O bonds from carbonyl groups and C-O bonds from alkoxy groups inherent to the material [59]. In contrast, the spectra of electrodes incorporating carbon quantum dots (GQDs), specifically GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB (Figures S3b-S3e), also display the -OH-related band in the 3600–3000 cm⁻¹ range. However, this band is less intense, while the vibrational bands in the 1700 and 1400 cm⁻¹ regions exhibit increased intensity, indicating a higher presence of functional groups such as carbonyl (C=O) and alkoxy (C-O), characteristic of GQDs [60]. Finally, the electrodes subjected to charge and discharge cycles in the supercapacitors (Figures S4c-S4f) show a re-establishment of the -O-H band intensity in the 3600–3000 cm⁻¹ range. Moreover, new bands emerge between 1700 and 900 cm⁻¹, corresponding to the formation of additional chemical species such as peroxides (C-O-C), carboxyl groups (-COOH), and esters (O-C=O), alongside the existing carbonyl (C=O) and alkoxy (CO) bands [61]. The generation of these new chemical species is attributed to the interaction of the materials with the electrolyte and the flow of electrical current through the electrodes during device operation.

The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) characterization presented in Fig. 12 provides detailed information about the chemical states of the species present in the electrodes, both before and after their utilization in SC devices. Fig. 12a-12b corresponds to the analysis of the O1s orbital for the GOcom+GQDscom and GOLIB+GQDsLIB electrodes, respectively, prior to their incorporation into the devices. In both figures, two signals are observed. For GOcom+GQDscom, these are centered at 534.0 eV and 532.6 eV, while for GOLIB+GQDsLIB, the signals are located at 533.8 eV and 532.5 eV. These signals are associated with OC/OH [62] and O = C bonds [63], respectively, which correspond to the oxidized surface of graphene oxide and the presence of GQDs. Additionally, the signals are similar in position and exhibit a comparable height ratio, indicating a similar chemical composition.

On the other hand, the O1s orbital spectra for the electrodes used in the SCs, shown in Fig. 12c and d, also display two signals. For the GOcom+GQDscom electrode, these are located at 531.9 eV and 533.2 eV, while for the GOLIB+GQDsLIB electrode, they are centered at 531.8 eV and 533.3 eV. The signals at 531.9 eV and 531.8 eV are associated with the previously mentioned O=C bond [63]. Comparing these signals with those in Fig. 12a and b, it is revealed a clear decrease in the intensity of the band, indicating a chemical transformation into another species. This observation is further supported by the signals at 533.2 eV and 533.3 eV, which exhibited increased intensity. These signals correspond to a group of newly formed chemical species during the electrochemical tests, including OC, CO-C, COOH [64] and oxygen vacancies (OV) [65]. Those functional groups are reported as crucial for energy storage since they create on the surface of the SC electrodes active sites for the charge storage. The presence of all these species are corroborated by the FTIR spectra previously discussed in Figure S4.

This study demonstrates the successful development and characterization of flexible supercapacitors (SCs) devices using graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from commercial graphite (GOcom) and recycled graphite from lithium-ion batteries (GOLIB). The synergistic interaction between GO and GQDs significantly enhances the electrochemical performance of the devices as evidenced by capacitance values reaching up to 2130 F/g, energy densities of 125 Wh/kg, for GOLIB+GQDs-SC device and 2519.1 F/g and 126 Wh/kg, for GOcom+GQDs-SC. Also, the devices exhibit exceptional stability under mechanical stress (above 80 % after 500 bending cycles) and extended cycling (above 88 % of capacitance retention after 1000 charge/discharge cycles). Surface chemical analysis through FTIR and XPS confirmed the presence of functional groups and defects such as oxygen vacancies that play a pivotal role in improving charge storage and ion transport properties. The increased ID/IG ratios observed in Raman spectroscopy further corroborate the contribution of structural defects to the exceptional electrochemical behavior. Additionally, the formation of oxygen vacancies during electrochemical cycling was identified as a key factor in enhancing the interaction between the electrode surface and electrolyte ions. Thus, this work addresses a pressing global challenge: the reduction of carbon waste generated by end-of-life lithium-ion batteries. It is well-known that improper disposal of battery components, such as electrolytes and electrode materials, poses significant ecological risks. With the global shift towards electromobility, ensuring that batteries can be reused or repurposed after their operational lifespan is crucial to mitigating environmental hazards. This study highlights the potential of reusing graphite from spent batteries to create high-performance energy storage materials, demonstrating a sustainable approach that aligns with global efforts to promote a circular economy. The results also highlight the importance of sustainable practices by demonstrating that recycled graphite from lithium-ion batteries can be effectively utilized to produce advanced energy storage materials such as graphene oxide and GQDs. The incorporation of GQDs into the electrodes not only improves conductivity but also promotes the formation of active sites critical for energy storage applications. In conclusion, the combination of GO and GQDs provides a viable pathway for the design of high-performance, flexible energy storage devices. This work sets a basis for future research into scalable, sustainable, and high-efficiency energy storage systems, paving the way for their potential use in portable electronics and other flexible applications.
